does: This paper introduces the concept of receiver-gated response laws as a framework for understanding control across biological and artificial systems. Rather than simply identifying order-inducing structures, the authors propose that true control requires a specific mathematical operator that maps material state, action/drive, bath conditions, and receiver state to response outcomes. They identify and characterize these response laws empirically across diverse systems: mouse anterior lateral motor cortex, C. elegans neurons, zebrafish neural circuits, and large language models with adapters. The core contribution is demonstrating that control operates through local, context-dependent mechanisms where interventions produce different effects (admission, saturation, sign-reversal, leakage, or overdrive) depending on the medium and system state, and validating this framework achieves 72.8-73.7% accuracy in predicting LLM response vector component signs across multiple material conditions.
connects: This work responds to a growing body of research in AI alignment, interpretability, and neural perturbation studies that has focused on identifying order-inducing structures in both artificial and biological systems. It builds on decades of neuroscience work characterizing neural response properties and control mechanisms, extending classical control theory concepts to domains where receiver state and bath conditions fundamentally modulate responses. The paper engages with recent efforts to understand how neural circuits and language models respond to interventions, connecting empirical neuroscience methods (recordings from mouse, C. elegans, and zebrafish) with modern interpretability techniques applied to transformer-based models. It synthesizes these threads by proposing that the apparent order found in previous studies reflects a deeper principle about how receiving systems gate and modulate external drives.
enables: This framework opens new directions for understanding control in both biological and artificial systems by moving beyond static descriptions of order to dynamic principles of responsiveness. It suggests methods for predicting how neural circuits and language models will respond to novel interventions by characterizing their receiver-gated response laws under different bath conditions. The work enables more precise neural steering and AI control strategies by identifying when interventions will succeed, fail, or produce unexpected effects based on system state. It points toward developing robust alignment techniques that account for context-dependent responses rather than assuming fixed control relationships, and suggests cross-domain principles that could unify understanding of biological and artificial intelligence. Future work could leverage these response laws to design more effective perturbation experiments, develop adaptive control strategies, and build more interpretable AI systems.
